57d9cc53b64d42b1.summary: Mean of grouped data: Direct (x̄ = Σfx/Σf), Assumed Mean (x̄ = a + Σfd/Σf), Step-Deviation (x̄ = a + h×Σfu/Σf). Use step-deviation when class width is constant for easiest calculation.

chunks.title: Understanding Mean of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**Mean of Grouped Data** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Methods for grouped mean:**

1. **Direct:** x̄ = Σfᵢxᵢ/Σfᵢ (xᵢ = class mark)
2. **Assumed Mean:** x̄ = a + Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ (dᵢ = xᵢ-a)
3. **Step-Deviation:** x̄ = a + h×Σfᵢuᵢ/Σfᵢ (uᵢ = (xᵢ-a)/h)

All give the SAME answer.

**Key points to remember:**
• Class mark = (LCL+UCL)/2
• Step-deviation fastest for equal-width classes
• All three methods yield identical results
• Arrange in table: class, x, f, fx or u, fu

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Mean of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Direct: Σfx/Σf
• Assumed mean: choose a near middle
• Step-deviation: h = class width
• Step-deviation simplifies calculations

**2. Important Formulas:**
Direct: x̄ = Σfx/Σf
Assumed: x̄ = a + Σfd/Σf
Step-Deviation: x̄ = a + h×Σfu/Σf

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Classes: 0-10(2), 10-20(5), 20-30(8), 30-40(4)
Direct: Σfx=10+75+200+140=425, Σf=19, x̄=425/19=22.37

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Wrong class mark:** Class mark = (LCL+UCL)/2

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Use step-deviation:** Fastest for equal-width classes

a8e85e46ba25fb68.summary: Mode of grouped data: Modal class has highest frequency. Mode = l + (f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂)×h. l = lower limit of modal class, f₁ = frequency of modal class, f₀ and f₂ = preceding and following frequencies.

chunks.title: Understanding Mode of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**Mode of Grouped Data** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Modal class** = class with highest frequency.

**Formula:**
Mode = l + (f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂) × h

Where:
l = lower limit of modal class
h = class width
f₁ = freq of modal class
f₀ = freq of class BEFORE modal
f₂ = freq of class AFTER modal

**Key points to remember:**
• Modal class = highest f
• f₀ is preceding, f₂ is following
• Mode ≠ class mark necessarily
• Identifies most frequent value

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Mode of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Mode is most frequent value
• Interpolates within modal class
• Mode ≠ mean or median
• Used for categorical preferences

**2. Important Formulas:**
Mode = l + (f₁-f₀)/(2f₁-f₀-f₂) × h

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
0-10(3), 10-20(8), 20-30(12), 30-40(6), 40-50(2)
Modal: 20-30. l=20, f₁=12, f₀=8, f₂=6, h=10.
Mode=20+(12-8)/(24-8-6)×10=20+4=24.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Wrong f₀/f₂:** f₀ is BEFORE, f₂ is AFTER modal class

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Identify modal class first:** Class with highest frequency

a631a4a22abd33fb.summary: Median of grouped data: Build cumulative frequency, find n/2, identify median class (first cf ≥ n/2). Median = l + (n/2-cf)/f × h. l = lower limit of median class, cf = cumulative freq before median class.

chunks.title: Understanding Median of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**Median of Grouped Data** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Steps:**
1. Build cumulative frequency (cf) column
2. Find n/2
3. Median class = first class where cf ≥ n/2
4. Apply formula:

**Median = l + (n/2 - cf)/f × h**

l = lower limit of median class
cf = cumulative freq BEFORE median class
f = freq of median class
h = class width

**Key points to remember:**
• Median = 50th percentile
• Find median class using n/2
• cf BEFORE median class (not including)
• Also findable via ogive

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Median of Grouped Data
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Less than ogive: upward sloping
• More than ogive: downward sloping
• Median from ogive: horizontal at n/2 to curve, then vertical
• Median less affected by outliers

**2. Important Formulas:**
Median = l + (n/2-cf)/f × h

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Classes: 10-20(5), 20-30(8), 30-40(12), 40-50(10), 50-60(5)
cf:5,13,25,35,40. n/2=20. Median class:30-40.
l=30, cf=13, f=12, h=10. Med=30+(20-13)/12×10=35.83.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **cf vs f:** cf = cumulative freq BEFORE median class, not including it

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Build cf table first:** Before finding median class

67512a99b49cf2e1.summary: Empirical relationship: 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean. Ogive (cumulative frequency curve) gives median graphically. Less-than and more-than ogives intersect at the median.

chunks.title: Understanding Graphical Representation of Cumulative Frequency Distribution
chunks.content:
**Graphical Representation of Cumulative Frequency Distribution** is a key concept in Statistics of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Ogive (Cumulative Frequency Curve):**
• **Less than ogive:** Plot upper limits vs cf (cumulative freq from start)
• **More than ogive:** Plot lower limits vs cf (cumulative freq from end)
• Median is the x-coordinate where y = n/2 meets the ogive
• Two ogives intersect at the median

**Empirical Relationship:**
3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
(Approximate, for moderately skewed distributions)

**Key points to remember:**
• Less than: slopes upward
• More than: slopes downward
• Intersection gives median
• Empirical formula is approximate

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Graphical Representation of Cumulative Frequency Distribution
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• From ogive: draw horizontal at n/2 to curve, then vertical to x-axis
• Empirical: 3 Med = Mode+2Mean
• Use when one measure unknown
• Helps cross-check all three measures

**2. Important Formulas:**
3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean
Mean = (3Med-Mode)/2
Mode = 3Med-2Mean
Median = (Mode+2Mean)/3

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
If Median=28, Mode=24: Mean = (84-24)/2 = 30.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** If Mean=25 and Median=24, find Mode using empirical relationship.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** 3 Med = Mode + 2 Mean
**Step 2:** 72 = Mode + 50
**Step 3:** Mode = 22

Therefore, Mode = 22.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Exact vs approximate:** Empirical formula is approximate

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **3 Med = Mode+2Mean:** Memorize this

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Mean=30, Median=28. Find Mode.

**Answer:** Mode=3×28-2×30=84-60=24.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Median=35, Mode=29. Find Mean.

**Answer:** Mean=(105-29)/2=38.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
From ogive, median=34. If mode=30, estimate mean.

**Answer:** 3×34=30+2M → M=36.
